crops.name: Cassava

forest.notes: Can be planted at the start of either rainy season, very flexible

savanna.notes: Plant early in the rains to establish before the dry season

sahel.notes: Generally not suited to the Sahel due to insufficient rainfall and short growing season

soil.type: Well-drained sandy-loam soil; tolerates poor soils better than most crops
soil.testing_tip: Cassava grows in soils too poor for maize. Avoid waterlogged or heavy clay soils as they cause root rot.

fertilizer.basal: NPK 15:15:15 at 100kg per hectare if soil is very poor, often not necessary on fertile land
fertilizer.topdress: Potassium-rich fertilizer (e.g. muriate of potash) at 3 months improves tuber size
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Cassava is often grown with no fertilizer at all on fairly fertile land; compost helps on degraded soils

watering.frequency: Rain-fed; cassava is highly drought tolerant once established
watering.critical_stages: First 2-3 months after planting need reasonable moisture for stem cuttings to root and sprout

common_pests_diseases.name: Cassava mosaic virus
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use disease-free stem cuttings from certified sources, remove and destroy infected plants

common_pests_diseases.name: Cassava mealybug
common_pests_diseases.sign: White cottony masses on stems and leaves, leaf curling
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use resistant varieties, encourage natural predators, avoid moving infected cuttings to new fields

common_pests_diseases.name: Root rot
common_pests_diseases.sign: Tubers soft and rotten when dug up
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Improve drainage, avoid planting in waterlogged spots

harvest_timeline.days_to_maturity: 8-18 months depending on variety, can be left in ground longer as a 'food bank'
harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Lower leaves yellow and drop, tubers reach desired size when test-dug
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Cut stem near ground, dig around the base carefully and pull up the tuber cluster by hand or with a hoe

beginner_tips: Use healthy, disease-free stem cuttings about 25-30cm long, plant at a slight angle with 2/3 of the cutting buried
Cassava can stay in the ground for many months past maturity, useful as a food reserve, but leaving too long can make tubers fibrous
Great first crop for poor soils since it requires minimal input and is very forgiving of mistakes

crops.name: Yam

forest.notes: Planted at the start of dry season (Nov-Dec) in some areas for early yam, or with first rains (March)

savanna.notes: Plant at the onset of rains, mounds should be prepared before rains start

soil.type: Deep, loose, well-drained loamy soil - yams need soft soil to allow tuber expansion
soil.testing_tip: Push a stick into the soil 30cm deep - if it goes in easily without much resistance, the soil is loose enough. Heavy clay produces misshapen tubers.

fertilizer.basal: Well-rotted manure or compost mixed into the mound before planting, about 2-3kg per mound
fertilizer.topdress: NPK 15:15:15 at 6-8 weeks after sprouting, applied around the base of the mound
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Yams traditionally grow well with just compost-rich mounds and crop rotation with legumes the prior season

watering.frequency: Rain-fed; needs consistent moisture especially in the first 2-3 months
watering.critical_stages: Sprouting and early vine establishment - dry spells here delay growth significantly

common_pests_diseases.name: Yam beetle
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Clear and burn bush residue before planting to reduce beetle populations, rotate fields

common_pests_diseases.name: Anthracnose
common_pests_diseases.sign: Dark lesions on leaves and stems, vine dieback
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use resistant varieties, avoid overcrowding for better airflow

common_pests_diseases.name: Nematodes
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Crop rotation, use of clean planting material (seed yams)

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Vines and leaves turn yellow and dry up
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Carefully dig around the mound by hand to avoid damaging the tuber, lift gently to preserve it for storage

beginner_tips: Mound or ridge preparation is the most important step - mounds should be at least 1m high and wide for good tuber development
Stake the vines with sticks so they climb - this improves light exposure and yield significantly
Use small 'seed yams' (cut pieces of tuber with a bud) as planting material, cure cut pieces in shade for a few days before planting to prevent rot

crops.name: Sweet Potato

forest.notes: Two planting windows possible due to bimodal rainfall

sahel.notes: Short-season varieties recommended, plant once rains are established

soil.type: Sandy-loam, well-drained soil; heavy clay produces poor-shaped tubers
soil.testing_tip: Similar test to yam - soil should be loose enough for tubers to swell without restriction. Ridging the soil before planting helps on heavier soils.

fertilizer.basal: Light application of NPK 15:15:15 at planting, sweet potato doesn't need heavy nitrogen (too much leaf growth, fewer tubers)
fertilizer.topdress: Potassium-rich fertilizer improves tuber quality, apply around 6 weeks after planting
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Compost worked into ridges before planting vine cuttings

watering.frequency: Rain-fed; drought tolerant but yields better with consistent moisture
watering.critical_stages: First 2-3 weeks after planting vine cuttings, to establish roots

common_pests_diseases.name: Sweet potato weevil
common_pests_diseases.sign: Tunnels and bitter taste in tubers
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Practice crop rotation, hill up soil around base to prevent weevil access, harvest promptly at maturity

common_pests_diseases.name: Leaf-eating caterpillars
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Hand-pick, use neem-based sprays if severe

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Leaves start yellowing, test-dig a few plants to check tuber size
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Loosen soil around the ridge with a hoe and lift tubers carefully to avoid cuts that cause rot in storage

beginner_tips: Grown from vine cuttings, not seeds - take 25-30cm cuttings from healthy vines and plant most of the cutting into ridged soil
Plant on ridges or mounds, not flat ground, for best tuber formation
Harvest promptly once mature - leaving tubers in the ground too long invites weevil damage

crops.name: Cocoyam (Taro/Tania)

forest.notes: Thrives in the humid forest zone with reliable rainfall

savanna.notes: Needs more consistent moisture than cassava or yam, plant with reliable rains

soil.type: Rich, moisture-retentive loamy soil - cocoyam likes more water than yam or cassava
soil.testing_tip: Cocoyam does well near streams or low-lying moist areas where other tubers might rot. If your land stays slightly damp after rain, that's ideal.

fertilizer.basal: Compost or well-rotted manure mixed into planting holes
fertilizer.topdress: NPK 15:15:15 at 2 and 4 months after planting if growth is slow
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Mulching with dried leaves around plants helps retain the moisture cocoyam needs

watering.frequency: Needs more consistent moisture than other tubers; supplement with watering during dry spells if possible
watering.critical_stages: Throughout growth - cocoyam is sensitive to prolonged drought unlike cassava or yam

common_pests_diseases.name: Taro leaf blight
common_pests_diseases.sign: Large brown blotches on leaves that spread quickly in wet weather
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Improve spacing for airflow, remove and destroy infected leaves promptly

common_pests_diseases.name: Aphids
common_pests_diseases.sign: Clusters of small insects on young leaves, curling
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Wash off with water spray, neem extract for persistent infestations

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Older leaves yellow and die back, corms reach harvestable size
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Dig carefully around the base to lift the main corm and surrounding cormels without bruising

beginner_tips: Plant cormels (small side tubers) with the growing tip facing up
Choose a slightly shaded or moist part of your farm if possible - full sun with no irrigation can stress the plant
Leaves are also edible (cooked, never raw) and are a useful side income/food source while tubers mature
